steps.title: Understand what the sample tells us
steps.content: 87% of a random sample of 200 residents said they are satisfied. A sample proportion estimates the population proportion but is not guaranteed to equal it exactly.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement I
steps.content: Statement I says 87% of ALL town residents would say they are satisfied. This cannot be concluded with certainty from a sample — due to sampling variability the true population percentage could differ. Statement I does NOT must be true.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement II
steps.content: Statement II says a second random sample of 200 would also yield exactly 87%. Sampling variability means different samples give different results; there is no reason the second must match the first exactly. Statement II does NOT must be true.

steps.title: Eliminate choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Neither: Neither statement must be true based on a single sample. ✓
  • Choice B — I only: Statement I cannot be concluded as a certainty from sample data alone. ✗
  • Choice C — II only: Statement II cannot be guaranteed due to sampling variability. ✗
  • Choice D — I and II: Both statements have the same flaw; neither must be true. ✗
A is correct.
